h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

scored.statedInsightEvidence: Good, the strip can carry that rule while copying.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner will continue to practice applying the food-to-eater arrow rule with the tutor's guidance.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor maintains a consistent supportive strategy of reinforcing the rule without any major pedagogical shift or reversal.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no pivot learner turn for the tutor to adapt to.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: There is no pivot learner turn for the tutor to adapt to.

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor maintains a consistent supportive strategy of reinforcing the rule without any major pedagogical shift or reversal.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The tutor maintains a consistent supportive strategy of reinforcing the rule without any major pedagogical shift or reversal.

scored.statedInsightEvidence: Fine, that label can stay about the source.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner will continue to identify the sources of the forces acting on the cart as prompted by the tutor.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor does not alter their strategy as the learner cooperates with the task.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no pivot learner turn or subsequent tutor turn to adapt to.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: There is no pivot learner turn or subsequent tutor turn to adapt to.

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor does not alter their strategy as the learner cooperates with the task.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The tutor does not alter their strategy as the learner cooperates with the task.

scored.recoheredEarlier: What I don’t see is why “side scale” and “perimeter scale” need separate labels if they both come out 3.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: So this 3 is not automatically the same piece of evidence twice.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner expects the tutor to justify why the two scales are mathematically different or to agree that the labels are redundant.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Put the claim on the counter: same number, same label. In this enlargement, yes: side scale is 3, perimeter scale is 3. Before the label goes on, what is each 3 comparing?
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor shifts the focus from the numerical result to the physical comparison being made, using the pencil and grid to show the difference in evidence.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Call it fussy. On the submitted worksheet, the label is the record of the test. Write both 3s, but give each one its own check.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor validates the learner's perspective by adopting their word 'fussy' and illustrating the practical necessity of the labels for a grader.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: What I don’t see is why “side scale” and “perimeter scale” need separate labels if they both come out 3.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: Call it fussy. On the submitted worksheet, the label is the record of the test. Write both 3s, but give each one its own check.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: The tutor validates the learner's perspective by adopting their word 'fussy' and illustrating the practical necessity of the labels for a grader.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: Put the claim on the counter: same number, same label. In this enlargement, yes: side scale is 3, perimeter scale is 3. Before the label goes on, what is each 3 comparing?
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor shifts the focus from the numerical result to the physical comparison being made, using the pencil and grid to show the difference in evidence.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: Put the claim on the counter: same number, same label. In this enlargement, yes: side scale is 3, perimeter scale is 3. Before the label goes on, what is each 3 comparing?
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The tutor shifts the focus from the numerical result to the physical comparison being made, using the pencil and grid to show the difference in evidence.

scored.recoheredEarlier: the same square has just been stretched both ways.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: All right, that top block is the bit I was sliding past, but it is the same old square again.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner expects a simple explanation of where their 'double' argument failed and will likely ask for a formula or rule to resolve the conflict.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Do not use the formula yet. Count with that block.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor actively blocks the use of formulas and shifts the representation to direct spatial counting to address the learner's conceptual breakdown.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Careful with “always”. Read the tiled square first.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor directly addresses the learner's generalization about rows and columns by pausing to analyze the tiled square before formulating a rule.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: the same square has just been stretched both ways.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: Careful with “always”. Read the tiled square first.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: The tutor directly addresses the learner's generalization about rows and columns by pausing to analyze the tiled square before formulating a rule.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: Do not use the formula yet. Count with that block.
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor actively blocks the use of formulas and shifts the representation to direct spatial counting to address the learner's conceptual breakdown.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: Do not use the formula yet. Count with that block.
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The tutor actively blocks the use of formulas and shifts the representation to direct spatial counting to address the learner's conceptual breakdown.
